perturbation.original_text: MMMW will provide on-call service for the Technical Infrastructure during the term of this Agreement from 7:00am to 5:00pm CST, with a guaranteed response time of 3 hours with a goal of on site within 2 hours. Support to include 7X24 hour pager support for emergency services.
perturbation.changed_text: MMMW will endeavor to provide on-call service for the Technical Infrastructure during the term of this Agreement from 7:00am to 5:00pm CST, with a possible response time of approximately 3 hours. Support may include 7X24 hour pager support for emergency services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will provide" to "will endeavor to provide" weakens the obligation. Replacing "guaranteed response time" with "possible response time of approximately 3 hours" makes the response time uncertain. Replacing "Support to include" with "Support may include" makes the 7X24 pager support discretionary, creating ambiguity and potentially leaving the customer without guaranteed emergency support.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Indiana Code § 26-1-2-313 because the original text established an express warranty concerning the on-call service and response time. By weakening the language to 'endeavor to provide' and 'possible response time,' MMMW is arguably attempting to disclaim or significantly reduce the scope of this express warranty, which may not be permissible under Indiana law if it's done in a way that deprives the buyer of the benefit of the bargain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the express warranty established in the original text, potentially contradicting Indiana Code § 26-1-2-313 as interpreted by the LLM Law Explanation. The change from 'will provide' to 'will endeavor to provide' and 'guaranteed response time' to 'possible response time' reduces the certainty and enforceability of the service commitment. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-313 (1)(a), states that 'Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise.' The original text's guarantees likely formed part of the bargain, and weakening those guarantees could be seen as diminishing the express warranty, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Customer shall provide a suitable environment for the Technical Infrastructure in accordance with MMMW specifications of non-humid environment, between the temperature range of 60 degrees F and 80 degrees F.
perturbation.changed_text: The Customer should attempt to provide a generally acceptable environment for the Technical Infrastructure, taking into account MMMW's suggestions for a non-humid environment and a comfortable temperature range.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall provide" with "should attempt to provide" weakens the obligation. "Generally acceptable environment" and "comfortable temperature range" are vague terms, replacing the specific requirements of a non-humid environment and a temperature range of 60-80 degrees F. This ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce the environmental requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Warranty of Fitness for a Particular Purpose under Indiana Code § 26-1-2-315
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the environmental conditions vague, the modified text could contradict the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose under Indiana Code § 26-1-2-315. If MMMW knows the customer is relying on them to ensure the technical infrastructure functions properly, and the infrastructure requires specific environmental conditions, making those conditions merely 'suggestions' creates ambiguity that could allow MMMW to argue they aren't responsible if the infrastructure fails due to unsuitable, but 'generally acceptable,' conditions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-315. Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-315. Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose. Where the seller at the time of contracting has reason to know any particular purpose for which the goods are required and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or judgment to select or furnish suitable goods, there is unless excluded or modified under the next section an implied warranty that the goods shall be fit for such purpose. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the firm "shall provide" with the weaker "should attempt to provide," and replaces specific environmental requirements with vague terms like "generally acceptable environment" and "comfortable temperature range." This contradicts the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Where the seller at the time of contracting has reason to know any particular purpose for which the goods are required and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or judgment to select or furnish suitable goods, there is unless excluded or modified under the next section an implied warranty that the goods shall be fit for such purpose." By weakening the environmental requirements, MMMW could argue they aren't responsible if the infrastructure fails due to unsuitable conditions, even if those conditions are deemed "generally acceptable." This undermines the customer's reliance on MMMW to ensure the technical infrastructure functions properly under specific environmental conditions.

perturbation.original_text: Charges will be invoiced and are payable within thirty (30) days after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Charges will be invoiced and are generally expected to be paid within a reasonable timeframe after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "payable within thirty (30) days" to "generally expected to be paid within a reasonable timeframe" introduces significant ambiguity regarding the payment deadline. The term 'reasonable timeframe' is subjective and unenforceable, removing a clear and specific payment obligation. What constitutes 'reasonable' is now open to interpretation and dispute.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change may contradict Indiana Code § 26-1-2-309(1) concerning the absence of specific time provisions. While this statute allows for a 'reasonable time' when an agreement is silent, the original agreement *did* specify a time (30 days). By removing the definite 30-day period and replacing it with 'reasonable timeframe', the modification creates uncertainty where the original contract was clear, potentially undermining the enforceability of the payment term. A court may still imply a reasonable time, but the ambiguity introduced can lead to disputes and is less certain than the original 30-day provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 26-1-2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination :: 2022 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code 2022 Indiana Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Indiana Code Title 26. Commercial Law Article 1. Uniform Commercial Code Chapter 2. Sales 26-1-2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination Universal Citation: IN Code § 26-1-2-309 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract, if not provided in IC 26-1-2 or agreed upon, shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration, it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one (1) party, except on the happening of an agreed event, requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party, and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Formerly: Acts 1963, c.317, s.2-309. As amended by P.L.152-1986, SEC.132. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modification replaces a specific payment deadline of "thirty (30) days" with a vague "reasonable timeframe," creating an ambiguous legal obligation. Indiana Code § 26-1-2-309(1) states that if a time for action under a contract is 'not provided' or 'agreed upon', a 'reasonable time' applies. However, the original contract *did* provide a specific time. By removing this specific time, the changed text introduces ambiguity where clarity existed, potentially undermining the enforceability of the payment term. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that 'reasonable timeframe' is subjective and unenforceable, removing a clear payment obligation. The Law Explanation states this undermines the enforceability of the payment term. Therefore, the change contradicts the intent of maintaining a definite and enforceable agreement.
